Workshop on Quantitative Literacy and Statistics: Session 4 - Case Study

Important: Before Start

  1. Copy Excel File sample_data_1.xlsx to a known folder in your computer
  2. Open R Studio and create a new R file in the folder where you copied the Excel File
  3. In R Studio: Click Session tab \(\rightarrow\) Click Set Working Directory \(\rightarrow\) Click Source File Location

Required Packages

suppressMessages({
if(!require(readxl)){install.packages("readxl")}
if(!require(corrplot)){install.packages("corrplot")}
if(!require(knitr)){install.packages("knitr")}
if(!require(ggplot2)){install.packages("ggplot2")}
if(!require(rpart)){install.packages("rpart")}
if(!require(rpart.plot)){install.packages("rpart.plot")}
if(!require(randomForest)){install.packages("randomForest")}
if(!require(caret)){install.packages("caret")}
if(!require(factoextra)){install.packages("factoextra")}
if(!require(FactoMineR)){install.packages("FactoMineR")}
if(!require(plotly)){install.packages("plotly")}
if(!require(DataExplorer)){install.packages("DataExplorer")}
if(!require(gbm)){install.packages("gbm")}
  

  

library("readxl")
library(corrplot)
library(knitr)
library(ggplot2)
library(rpart)
library(rpart.plot)
library(randomForest)
library(caret)
library(factoextra)
library(FactoMineR)
library(plotly)
library(gbm)
library(DataExplorer)
})

1 Brief Description of the Data Set: Mouse Model of Glaucoma

Data Provide By: Thompson, Jenny L <jennthompson@unmc.edu>

We use a mouse model of glaucoma. We’re seeking to uncover the influence of intraocular pressure (IOP) on different variables in a bilateral brain region that receives input from both eyes. The data are obtained from tissue that contains input from BOTH eyes, then run the computations with data from the LEFT eye and RIGHT eye separately.

Independent Variables:

  • AGE is categorized (4m, 9m, 12m) with groups relatively balanced, but I have exact values, so can be continuous

  • STRAIN is categorical (D2s get glaucoma, D2-Controls = same genetic background sans glaucoma mutation)

Dependent Variables:

  • IOP (intraocular pressure) is recorded in mmHg and is measured in each eye separately; these are taken monthly

  • IOP AUC I use the area under the curve for IOP so that I can maximize the use of the longitudinal data

  • PUNCTA DENSITY is a value telling the density of area covered by a fluorescent signal

  • BRANCH LENGTH is the average branching of microglial cells in the tissue

  • ENDPOINTS average number of endpoints of microglia in the tissue; derived from same output as

  • BRANCH LENGTH; these two variables do strongly correlate and I believe they should be combined into one variable (i.e. a “ramification factor”), but they do indicate different features, so I am in need of advice on this please

  • C1Q INTENSITY is the peak intensity of a fluorescent signal in the tissue

  • MICROGLIA COUNT number of microglia in 3d volume of tissue; manual counts

2 Data Inspection

2.1 Read Data Set: Mouse Model of Glaucoma

Load data set from the excel file named sample_data_1.xlsx. Here we create two data sets for experiment (or test) and control.

library("readxl")

glaucoma_experiment    = as.data.frame(read_excel("sample_data_1.xlsx", sheet = "D2"))
glaucoma_control       = as.data.frame(read_excel("sample_data_1.xlsx", sheet = "D2 Control"))

Let’s observe the first few observations of the experiment (or test) data where ‘Strain’ is D2/J.

head(glaucoma_experiment)
##   Age Strain Sex Microglia vGlut2_Puncta_Density Endpoints IOP_Right IOP_Left AUC_R AUC_L
## 1   4   D2/J   F        23              7.870000  138.8696      13.5     14.5   525   483
## 2   4   D2/J   F        24              7.470000  128.7500      13.5     14.5   378   378
## 3   4   D2/J   M        25              6.930000  124.9600      13.0     11.0   399   441
## 4   4   D2/J   M        22              8.880000  145.8636      12.5     12.5   399   441
## 5   4   D2/J   M        18              8.151936  166.3889      12.5     11.5   420   420
## 6   4   D2/J   M        23              8.349160  269.6957       9.0      9.0   216   546

Let’s observe the first few observations of the control data where ‘Strain’ is D2-C.

head(glaucoma_control)
##   Age Strain Sex Microglia vGlut2_Puncta_Density Endpoints IOP_Right IOP_Left AUC_R AUC_L
## 1   4   D2-C   F        26                 7.867   167.200       9.5     10.5   266   294
## 2   4   D2-C   M        24                 7.728   131.100      10.5     11.0   294   308
## 3   4   D2-C   F        22                 9.795   150.820      11.5     12.5   322   350
## 4   4   D2-C   M        21                 7.867   145.000      12.5     14.0   350   392
## 5   4   D2-C   M        18                 7.809   158.222      13.0     14.0   364   392
## 6   4   D2-C   M        14                 7.743   202.200      11.5     10.5   322   294

2.2 Merge Experiment and Control Data Set

For further analysis of data we combined the experiment data set and test data set using rbind function in R.

glaucoma_df = rbind(glaucoma_experiment, glaucoma_control)

Lets make another quick look at the data set and check the column names and data set size.

# First five observations
head(glaucoma_df, 5)
##   Age Strain Sex Microglia vGlut2_Puncta_Density Endpoints IOP_Right IOP_Left AUC_R AUC_L
## 1   4   D2/J   F        23              7.870000  138.8696      13.5     14.5   525   483
## 2   4   D2/J   F        24              7.470000  128.7500      13.5     14.5   378   378
## 3   4   D2/J   M        25              6.930000  124.9600      13.0     11.0   399   441
## 4   4   D2/J   M        22              8.880000  145.8636      12.5     12.5   399   441
## 5   4   D2/J   M        18              8.151936  166.3889      12.5     11.5   420   420
# Last observations
tail(glaucoma_df,5)
##    Age Strain Sex Microglia vGlut2_Puncta_Density Endpoints IOP_Right IOP_Left AUC_R AUC_L
## 71  12   D2-C   M        25              7.041636 136.76000      13.0     12.0  3703  3339
## 72  12   D2-C   F        22              7.655000 164.18182      14.5     14.0  4070  4087
## 73  12   D2-C   F        24              7.600000 123.91667      16.0     16.5  4071  4045
## 74  12   D2-C   F        33              7.334000  98.30303      10.0      8.0  3812  3457
## 75  12   D2-C   F        28              5.763331 117.92857      14.5     15.5  3953  3996
# Column names
colnames(glaucoma_df)
##  [1] "Age"                   "Strain"                "Sex"                   "Microglia"            
##  [5] "vGlut2_Puncta_Density" "Endpoints"             "IOP_Right"             "IOP_Left"             
##  [9] "AUC_R"                 "AUC_L"
# Size of the Data Set
print(dim(glaucoma_df))
## [1] 75 10

2.3 Modify the Data Set by Stacking for Left and Right Eyes

For this data set, restructuring is beneficial for data visualization and modeling.

IOP = stack(glaucoma_df[7:8])
colnames(IOP) = c("IOP_Values","IOP_Eye")

#AUC = stack(glaucoma_df[9:10])
#colnames(AUC) = c("AUC_Values","AUC_Eye")

glaucoma_stacked_df = cbind(glaucoma_df[1:6],IOP)

head(glaucoma_stacked_df, n = 10)
##    Age Strain Sex Microglia vGlut2_Puncta_Density Endpoints IOP_Values   IOP_Eye
## 1    4   D2/J   F        23              7.870000  138.8696       13.5 IOP_Right
## 2    4   D2/J   F        24              7.470000  128.7500       13.5 IOP_Right
## 3    4   D2/J   M        25              6.930000  124.9600       13.0 IOP_Right
## 4    4   D2/J   M        22              8.880000  145.8636       12.5 IOP_Right
## 5    4   D2/J   M        18              8.151936  166.3889       12.5 IOP_Right
## 6    4   D2/J   M        23              8.349160  269.6957        9.0 IOP_Right
## 7    4   D2/J   M        23              7.669832  166.0435        9.5 IOP_Right
## 8    4   D2/J   M        32              7.881665   96.7500        9.5 IOP_Right
## 9    4   D2/J   M        23              7.406866  140.9565       10.0 IOP_Right
## 10   4   D2/J   M        23              8.086194  154.6087       12.0 IOP_Right

3 Data Visualization

In this section, we will apply different data visualization techniques for the variables in the data set.

3.1 Bar Charts for Categorical Variables

  1. Bar Cart for Sex Category Counts
library(ggplot2)

ggplot(data=glaucoma_df, aes(x= Sex, fill = Sex)) +
  geom_bar(stat="count") + theme_minimal()

  1. Bar Cart for Strain Category Counts
ggplot(data=glaucoma_df, aes(x= Strain, fill = Strain)) +
  geom_bar(stat="count") + theme_minimal()

3.2 Scatter Plot

A scatter plot is drawing for IOP_Left and IOP_Right. The points are colored based on the Strain.

scatter_plot = ggplot(data=glaucoma_df, aes(x= IOP_Right, y = IOP_Left, color=Strain)) +
  geom_point() + theme_minimal()

3.3 Box Plots

We draw box plots by groups, thereby, we can observe the data distribution and compare with other groups.

  1. Box plots for IOP values by Eye Side and by Strain
ggplot(data=glaucoma_stacked_df, aes(x= IOP_Eye, y = IOP_Values, fill = Strain)) +
  geom_boxplot()+ theme_minimal()

  1. Box plots for IOP values by Strain and by Eye Side
ggplot(data=glaucoma_stacked_df, aes(x= Strain, y = IOP_Values, fill = IOP_Eye)) +
  geom_boxplot()+ theme_minimal()

  1. Box plots for IOP values by Age and by Eye Side
ggplot(data=glaucoma_stacked_df, aes(x= as.factor(Age), y = IOP_Values, fill = IOP_Eye  )) +
  geom_boxplot() + xlab('Age')+ theme_minimal()

3.4 Density Plots

Density plots help us to understand the data distribution. Here the density plot are drawn by Strain, Age groups.

  1. Density plots for IOP values by Strain and by Eye Side
ggplot(glaucoma_stacked_df, aes(x=IOP_Values, fill=IOP_Eye, color=IOP_Eye))+
                       geom_density(alpha=0.6) +
                       facet_wrap(~Strain) + 
                        theme_minimal()

  1. Density plots for IOP values by Age and by Eye Side
ggplot(glaucoma_stacked_df, aes(x=IOP_Values, fill=IOP_Eye, color=IOP_Eye))+
                       geom_density(alpha=0.6) +
                       facet_wrap(~as.factor(Age) ) + 
                        theme_minimal()

3.5 Correlation Plots

Here a correlation plot is drawn to understand the correlation between the variables in the data set.

library(corrplot)
#Get Correlations
correlation_df = cor(glaucoma_df[,-(2:3)]) # Remove Strain and Sex as they are categorical variables

#Plot Correlations
corrplot.mixed(correlation_df,
               lower = "number", 
               upper = "circle",
               tl.col = "black", tl.cex = 0.5
               )

The following correlation plot is is grouped by the Strain. (Note: Some data manipulation is done for this graph)

library(corrplot)
#Get Correlations
correlation_df = cor(glaucoma_df[,-(2:3)]) # Remove Strain and Sex as they are categorical variables

par(mfrow=c(1,2)) 

# Plot Correlation for each Strain
for(i1 in unique(glaucoma_df$Strain)) {
            glaucoma_df_i1 = glaucoma_df[glaucoma_df$Strain==i1,]
            correlation_df = cor(glaucoma_df_i1[,-(2:3)])
            corrplot.mixed(correlation_df,
               lower = "number", 
               upper = "circle", title = i1,
               tl.col = "black", tl.cex = 0.4,
               mar=c(0,0,2,0)
               )
           }

4 Unsupervised Learning: Clustering

Here we will apply clustering for the numerical variables in the data set.

To identify the samples in the data set, we renamed each sample by “Age_Strain_Sex_#”.

set.seed(123)
library(factoextra)

glaucoma_df_cluster = glaucoma_df[-c(1:3)]

rownames(glaucoma_df_cluster) = paste(glaucoma_df$Age,'_',glaucoma_df$Strain,'_',glaucoma_df$Sex,'_',rownames(glaucoma_df))

4.1 Identify the Number of Required Clusters

  • Silhouette method is used to identify the required number of clusters.
fviz_nbclust(glaucoma_df_cluster, kmeans, method = "silhouette")

4.2 Apply K-Means Clustering

set.seed(123)
k_means_clustering = kmeans(glaucoma_df_cluster, centers = 2)
fviz_cluster(k_means_clustering, data = glaucoma_df_cluster,labelsize = 5)

Based on the clustering plot, we could observe that clusters are mainly sepetered with respect to the Age. As the the first cluster representes ages 9-month and 12 month, where as ‘second’ cluster represents ages 4.

4.3 Apply Hierarchical Clustering

set.seed(123)
library(ggdendro)

df_distance = dist(glaucoma_df_cluster, method = 'euclidean')
h_clustering = hclust(df_distance)
ggdendrogram(h_clustering)

5 Supervised Learning

5.1 Apply Linear Regression Model for Explanation of Dependent Variables

With lm function, we can use anova function to obtain the effect from each variable.

glaucoma_linear_regression = lm(formula = IOP_Values ~ Age + Strain + Sex + IOP_Eye,data = glaucoma_stacked_df)
anova(glaucoma_linear_regression)
## Analysis of Variance Table
## 
## Response: IOP_Values
##            Df  Sum Sq Mean Sq F value    Pr(>F)    
## Age         1  467.50  467.50 30.4622 1.526e-07 ***
## Strain      1  857.71  857.71 55.8887 6.697e-12 ***
## Sex         1  367.95  367.95 23.9755 2.571e-06 ***
## IOP_Eye     1   19.80   19.80  1.2903    0.2579    
## Residuals 145 2225.29   15.35                      
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Here we can see that Age, Strain, and Sex are significant variables for the IOP. However, it appears there is no significance difference between IOP Left Eye and IOP Right Eye.

5.2 Apply Linear Regression Model with Interactions for Explanation of Dependent Variables

With lm function, we can use anova function to obtain the effect from each variable.

glaucoma_linear_regression = lm(formula = IOP_Values ~ (Age + Strain + Sex + IOP_Eye)^2,data = glaucoma_stacked_df)
anova(glaucoma_linear_regression)
## Analysis of Variance Table
## 
## Response: IOP_Values
##                 Df  Sum Sq Mean Sq F value    Pr(>F)    
## Age              1  467.50  467.50 36.3795 1.390e-08 ***
## Strain           1  857.71  857.71 66.7451 1.702e-13 ***
## Sex              1  367.95  367.95 28.6327 3.517e-07 ***
## IOP_Eye          1   19.80   19.80  1.5409 0.2165723    
## Age:Strain       1  191.06  191.06 14.8677 0.0001757 ***
## Age:Sex          1    0.89    0.89  0.0696 0.7922956    
## Age:IOP_Eye      1    0.95    0.95  0.0737 0.7864051    
## Strain:Sex       1  239.16  239.16 18.6105 3.022e-05 ***
## Strain:IOP_Eye   1    6.03    6.03  0.4692 0.4944879    
## Sex:IOP_Eye      1    0.97    0.97  0.0755 0.7838605    
## Residuals      139 1786.23   12.85                      
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

The interactions Age:Strain and Strain:Sex are significant

  • For different Age levels, the average IOP values are significantly different between Strains

  • When Sex is change from M to F, the average IOP values are significantly different between Strains

5.2.1 ANOVA - Interaction Plots

interaction.plot(x.factor = glaucoma_stacked_df$Age, 
                 trace.factor = glaucoma_stacked_df$Sex, 
                 response = glaucoma_stacked_df$IOP_Values,
                 fun = mean, 
                 xlab="Age", 
                 ylab="IOP Values", trace.label="Sex" ,
                 main = "Age:Sex Interaction Plot")

interaction.plot(x.factor = glaucoma_stacked_df$Strain, 
                 trace.factor = glaucoma_stacked_df$Sex, 
                 response = glaucoma_stacked_df$IOP_Values,
                 fun = mean, 
                 xlab="Strain", 
                 ylab="IOP Values", 
                 trace.label="Sex" ,
                 main = "Strain:Sex Interaction Plot")

5.3 Create Predictor Models

  • Response Variable: IOP Values
  • Dependent Variables: Age, Strain , Sex , IOP_Eye

5.3.1 Train-Test Split

Apply train-test split with 70% as train data set and 30% as test data set.

  • glaucoma_stacked_df_train - Training Data Set

  • glaucoma_stacked_df_test - Testing Data Set

set.seed(1234)
sample = sample(c(TRUE, FALSE), nrow(glaucoma_stacked_df), replace=TRUE, prob=c(0.7,0.3))
glaucoma_stacked_df_train  = glaucoma_stacked_df[sample, ]
glaucoma_stacked_df_test   = glaucoma_stacked_df[!sample, ]

5.3.2 Train the Linear Regression Model with Cross Validation

  • 4 Folds are applied for cross validation (This can be changed)
library(caret)

cross_validate_setup = trainControl(method = "cv", number = 4) # Cross validation with 4 folds

linear_regression_cv = train(IOP_Values ~ (Age + Strain + Sex + IOP_Eye)^2, 
                             data = glaucoma_stacked_df_train,                 
                             trControl = cross_validate_setup,           
                             method = "lm",
                             metric = 'RMSE'       # RMSE is used to compare the model
                     )

linear_regression_cv
## Linear Regression 
## 
## 112 samples
##   4 predictor
## 
## No pre-processing
## Resampling: Cross-Validated (4 fold) 
## Summary of sample sizes: 84, 85, 83, 84 
## Resampling results:
## 
##   RMSE      Rsquared   MAE     
##   3.907711  0.4828074  2.933547
## 
## Tuning parameter 'intercept' was held constant at a value of TRUE

5.3.3 Train the Random Forest Regression Model with Cross Validation

cross_validate_setup = trainControl(method = "cv", number = 4) # Cross validation with 4 folds

random_forest_regression_cv = train(IOP_Values ~ (Age + Strain + Sex + IOP_Eye), 
                             data = glaucoma_stacked_df_train,                 
                             trControl = cross_validate_setup,           
                             method = "rf",
                             metric = 'RMSE'       # RMSE is used to compare the model
                     )

random_forest_regression_cv
## Random Forest 
## 
## 112 samples
##   4 predictor
## 
## No pre-processing
## Resampling: Cross-Validated (4 fold) 
## Summary of sample sizes: 83, 85, 85, 83 
## Resampling results across tuning parameters:
## 
##   mtry  RMSE      Rsquared   MAE     
##   2     3.644183  0.5517190  2.660361
##   3     3.714248  0.5396859  2.675851
##   4     3.745871  0.5341173  2.655340
## 
## RMSE was used to select the optimal model using the smallest value.
## The final value used for the model was mtry = 2.

5.3.4 Train the Boosting Model with Cross Validation

cross_validate_setup = trainControl(method = "cv", number = 4) # Cross validation with 4 folds

boosting_regression_cv = train(IOP_Values ~ (Age + Strain + Sex + IOP_Eye), 
                             data = glaucoma_stacked_df_train,                 
                             trControl = cross_validate_setup,           
                             method = "gbm",
                             metric = 'RMSE'       # RMSE is used to compare the model
                     )
boosting_regression_cv
## Stochastic Gradient Boosting 
## 
## 112 samples
##   4 predictor
## 
## No pre-processing
## Resampling: Cross-Validated (4 fold) 
## Summary of sample sizes: 85, 83, 84, 84 
## Resampling results across tuning parameters:
## 
##   interaction.depth  n.trees  RMSE      Rsquared   MAE     
##   1                   50      3.965290  0.4761767  3.142700
##   1                  100      3.988170  0.4657997  3.223047
##   1                  150      3.976582  0.4663227  3.247917
##   2                   50      3.678288  0.5442205  2.937053
##   2                  100      3.615583  0.5580590  2.895679
##   2                  150      3.636920  0.5539942  2.899615
##   3                   50      3.654926  0.5465249  2.891683
##   3                  100      3.618513  0.5596161  2.909455
##   3                  150      3.627240  0.5571990  2.892020
## 
## Tuning parameter 'shrinkage' was held constant at a value of 0.1
## Tuning parameter
##  'n.minobsinnode' was held constant at a value of 10
## RMSE was used to select the optimal model using the smallest value.
## The final values used for the model were n.trees = 100, interaction.depth = 2, shrinkage = 0.1
##  and n.minobsinnode = 10.

5.3.5 Apply test data set to compare the performance of models

linear_regression_prediction = predict(linear_regression_cv,newdata = glaucoma_stacked_df_test)
random_forest_prediction     = predict(random_forest_regression_cv,newdata = glaucoma_stacked_df_test)
boosting_prediction          = predict(boosting_regression_cv,newdata = glaucoma_stacked_df_test)

linear_regression_rmse_test = RMSE(pred = linear_regression_prediction, obs = glaucoma_stacked_df_test$IOP_Values)

random_forest_rmse_test     = RMSE(pred = random_forest_prediction, obs = glaucoma_stacked_df_test$IOP_Values)

boosting_rmse_test          = RMSE(pred = boosting_prediction, obs = glaucoma_stacked_df_test$IOP_Values)


print(paste('RMSE of Test Data: Linear Regression = ', linear_regression_rmse_test))
## [1] "RMSE of Test Data: Linear Regression =  3.32033784086963"
print(paste('RMSE of Test Data: Random Forest Regression = ', random_forest_rmse_test))
## [1] "RMSE of Test Data: Random Forest Regression =  3.09650465557282"
print(paste('RMSE of Test Data: Boosting Regression = ', boosting_rmse_test))
## [1] "RMSE of Test Data: Boosting Regression =  3.10053564256255"

Random Forest Regression model has the lowest test RMSE; therefore, we can use that for prediction purpose.

6 Extra: Automated Exploratory Analysis Package: DataExplorer

library(DataExplorer)
create_report(glaucoma_stacked_df)
